paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 11: The National Day Orchid (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 3 girl who watches her grandmother plant yet another orchid on National Day morning at the corridor of their HDB flat, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
It was barely six on the ninth of August, and the corridor of our HDB flat in Ang Mo Kio smelled of damp earth. Ah Ma knelt beside the railing, easing a young orchid into a pot of soil. She did this every National Day. I had watched her for as long as I could remember, and I had never once asked her why.

Paragraph 2
'You woke up early,' she said, without turning around. The pinkish bloom in her hand caught the first light. I sat on the cold tiles and watched. Her fingers were lined and brown, but they moved with a steadiness that mine could not match. 'I will be in Sec 3 next week,' I said, and she gave a small laugh that ended too quickly.

Paragraph 3
Behind her, a long row of small empty terracotta pots stood stacked against the wall. I had counted them once, in Primary 6: fifty-two. 'Ah Ma,' I asked carefully, 'why do you always plant an orchid in August? None of them have ever lasted till December.' She pressed the soil down once, twice, before answering.

Paragraph 4
'In 1972,' she said, 'your Ah Gong wore an orchid pinned to his shirt at the National Day Parade. After the fireworks, on the bus going home, he asked me to marry him.' She did not look at me. 'Every year I plant one. Every year it dies. But every year, in August, I had a husband.' She made the last word sound like a small, ordinary thing, the way a person says 'I had breakfast.' My throat tightened in a way I did not know how to name.

Paragraph 5
At about ten, my brother Jun Hao wandered out and glanced at the new orchid. 'Ah Ma, this one also will die by November.' He said it lightly. I felt heat climb up the back of my neck. 'Don't be like that,' I snapped, louder than I meant to. Jun Hao raised both hands and walked back inside. Ah Ma said nothing, but the corner of her mouth lifted slightly, like a curtain caught by a small wind.

Paragraph 6
That afternoon at the kopitiam downstairs, we ate bee hoon under the ceiling fan. Ah Ma stirred her soup three times without taking a sip. I noticed for the first time that the small enamel orchid pin on the collar of her blouse — the one she always wore in August — was the same pink as the bloom now in the corridor pot upstairs.

Paragraph 7
In the evening we watched the NDP broadcast on TV. Ah Ma sat very still, her thumb travelling back and forth across the enamel pin. She was not really watching the screen. She was somewhere else — on a bus in 1972, beside a young man with a flower on his shirt. I pretended not to see, because there are some things a granddaughter must allow her Ah Ma to keep.

Paragraph 8
After everyone had gone to bed, I slipped out of my room. The corridor was lit only by the orange glow of the void deck lamps below. I knelt on the same patch of tile where Ah Ma had knelt that morning, and rested two fingers gently on the soil, patting it once — the way I had seen her do, fifty-two times, in pots that no longer existed. I did not water it. I did not say anything. But for the first time, the orchid did not feel only like Ah Ma's. It felt, in some small way, also like mine.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. HDB: Housing & Development Board public flats; Ah Ma / Ah Gong: maternal grandmother / grandfather in Hokkien; bee hoon: rice vermicelli; NDP: the National Day Parade on 9 August.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what does the phrase 'I had never once asked her why' suggest about the narrator's attitude towards Ah Ma's annual orchid planting up until that morning? [1]
questions.answer: she had always accepted it as an ordinary family routine and had never been curious enough about the reason behind it to question her grandmother

questions.stem: Q2(a). In Paragraph 2, what does the detail that Ah Ma's laugh 'ended too quickly' suggest about her? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests that something was weighing on her mind / she was not in a fully cheerful mood and was holding back deeper feelings

questions.stem: Q2(b). Which word in Paragraph 2 shows that Ah Ma's hands were practised and assured despite her age? [1]
Q3. In Paragraph 3, what is the significance of the fifty-two empty terracotta pots stacked against the wall? [1]
questions.answer: they represent the fifty-two orchids Ah Ma has planted every August over the years, all of which have died — they are a silent record of her unbroken yearly ritual

questions.stem: Q4. Explain what Ah Ma means in Paragraph 4 when she says, 'Every year it dies. But every year, in August, I had a husband.' [1]
questions.answer: she is saying that even though each orchid dies, the act of planting one every August lets her relive — even briefly — the year when her late husband proposed to her, so for that moment she has him back

questions.stem: Q5. What is effective about the comparison 'she made the last word sound like a small, ordinary thing, the way a person says I had breakfast' in Paragraph 4? [1]
questions.answer: the everyday comparison shows how Ah Ma is downplaying her grief and refusing to make it sound dramatic — the understatement makes her loss feel even more moving to the narrator

questions.stem: Q6. In Paragraph 5, why does the narrator snap at her brother Jun Hao 'louder than I meant to'? [1]
questions.answer: she now understands what the orchid means to Ah Ma, so her brother's careless teasing feels disrespectful — she reacts more strongly than she had intended because she has become protective of Ah Ma's ritual

questions.stem: Q7(a). What does the word 'fastidious'-style detail of Ah Ma stirring her soup 'three times without taking a sip' in Paragraph 6 reveal about her state of mind? [1]
questions.answer: she is preoccupied and lost in thought — she is going through the motions without really registering what she is doing, suggesting her mind is elsewhere, most likely on the memory of her husband

questions.stem: Q7(b). What does the narrator notice 'for the first time' in Paragraph 6, and why is this detail meaningful? [1]
questions.answer: she notices that the pink enamel orchid pin on Ah Ma's collar is the same pink as the new orchid in the corridor pot, suggesting that Ah Ma has been quietly carrying her husband's memory on her clothes as well as in the planting all along

questions.stem: Q8. In Paragraph 7, why does the narrator 'pretend not to see' Ah Ma's thumb moving over the enamel pin during the fireworks? [1]
questions.answer: she senses that Ah Ma is privately revisiting a precious memory and that being observed would embarrass her — she shows respect by allowing Ah Ma her private moment

questions.stem: Q9. What is the effect of the phrase 'in pots that no longer existed' at the end of Paragraph 8? [1]
questions.answer: it emphasises how many years and how many losses lie behind Ah Ma's gesture — the narrator is repeating an action that traces back through fifty-two earlier orchids, linking her to a long unseen history of devotion

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of ending the passage with 'But for the first time, the orchid did not feel only like Ah Ma's. It felt, in some small way, also like mine'? [1]
questions.answer: it shows that the narrator has stepped into Ah Ma's ritual and is now connected to her grandmother's love and loss — the family memory has quietly been passed down from one generation to the next

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling changes as National Day unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: curious / amused / moved / dismissive / protective / connected / bored / suspicious. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'National Day Orchid' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift across the day — from being curious as she finally asks Ah Ma why she plants an orchid every August, to being moved when Ah Ma explains the story of her late husband's proposal in 1972, to being protective when her brother teases Ah Ma about the doomed plant, ending connected late at night when she kneels at the corridor and pats the soil herself. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as she finally asks Ah Ma why she always plants an orchid in August, even though none of them have ever lasted till December? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4 as Ah Ma quietly tells her how Ah Gong proposed on the bus in 1972 with an orchid pinned to his shirt? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5 as her brother Jun Hao casually predicts that the new orchid will be dead by November and she snaps at him louder than she meant to? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she kneels alone in the corridor at night and pats the soil with two fingers — the same way she had seen Ah Ma do that morning? [1]
